quickStartTutorial: Players say what their characters try to do.
The DM decides whether the action simply works or requires a roll.
When a roll is needed, the player rolls a die, usually a d20.
The character sheet adds the needed bonuses.

faq.question: What is Dungeons & Dragons?
faq.shortAnswer: Dungeons & Dragons is a group storytelling game with rules. Players control characters, the DM describes the world, and dice help decide uncertain moments.

faq.question: What is a Dungeon Master?
faq.shortAnswer: The Dungeon Master, or DM, runs the game. The DM describes places, plays monsters and other people, applies rules, and explains what happens.

faq.question: What is a player?
faq.shortAnswer: A player controls one main character. The player says what that character tries to do and uses the character sheet when rolls or rules are needed.

faq.question: What is a party?
faq.shortAnswer: The party is the group of player characters. Most D&D games assume the party works together.

faq.question: What is a character sheet?
faq.shortAnswer: A character sheet is the record of your character. It lists your abilities, hit points, armor, skills, attacks, spells, equipment, and special features.

faq.question: What is D&D Beyond?
faq.shortAnswer: D&D Beyond is one possible tool for digital character sheets, character creation, campaign management, and rules lookup. This table will use it only if the DM chooses it.

faq.question: What is Discord used for?
faq.shortAnswer: Discord is used for voice chat, text chat, scheduling, announcements, recaps, and quick questions between sessions.

faq.question: What is Owlbear Rodeo?
faq.shortAnswer: Owlbear Rodeo is a simple virtual tabletop for maps and tokens. It helps everyone see where characters, enemies, and important places are.

faq.question: What is Roll20?
faq.shortAnswer: Roll20 is a larger virtual tabletop with maps, tokens, character sheet tools, and dice. This group only uses it if the DM says so.

faq.question: Do we need a map for everything?
faq.shortAnswer: No. Most scenes can be described through roleplay and narration. A world map may help show where the party is traveling. A battle map or floor plan is mainly useful when movement, distance, cover, or positioning matters during a fight.

faq.question: Will the DM need to build every town and house?
faq.shortAnswer: No. The DM can describe a town without constructing it. Digital maps, paper maps, miniatures, floor plans, or no map at all can all work depending on the scene.

faq.question: What is a battle map?
faq.shortAnswer: A battle map is a top-down view of a location used when positioning matters. It may show a room, road, forest clearing, tavern, bridge, cave, or other encounter area.

faq.question: What is theater of the mind?
faq.shortAnswer: Theater of the mind means the group imagines the scene from the DM's description instead of using a detailed map or miniatures.

faq.question: What do I need before my first session?
faq.shortAnswer: Join Discord, test your microphone, read the table rules, and ask questions. The DM will choose the character sheet and map tools before the campaign starts.

faq.question: What do I do on my turn?
faq.shortAnswer: Say what your character wants to do. In combat, you usually can move and take one main action, and some characters also have a bonus action.

faq.question: What is initiative?
faq.shortAnswer: Initiative decides turn order during combat. Everyone involved rolls, and higher results usually act earlier.

faq.question: What is Armor Class?
faq.shortAnswer: Armor Class, or AC, is the number an attack usually needs to meet or beat to hit you.

faq.question: What are hit points?
faq.shortAnswer: Hit points, or HP, show how much damage a character can take before going down.

faq.question: What happens if my character reaches 0 hit points?
faq.shortAnswer: Your character usually falls unconscious and may need death saving throws. The DM will walk you through the exact steps.

faq.question: What is a skill check?
faq.shortAnswer: A skill check is a roll to see whether your character succeeds at a trained or risky task, such as sneaking, climbing, noticing a clue, or persuading someone.

faq.question: What is a saving throw?
faq.shortAnswer: A saving throw is a defensive roll to resist danger, such as poison, a trap, a spell, or being knocked down.

faq.question: What is an attack roll?
faq.shortAnswer: An attack roll is a roll to see whether an attack hits. If it meets or beats the target's Armor Class, it usually hits.

faq.question: What is damage?
faq.shortAnswer: Damage lowers hit points. Different weapons, spells, and hazards use different dice for damage.

faq.question: What is advantage?
faq.shortAnswer: Advantage means you roll two d20s and use the higher result. It represents a helpful situation.

faq.question: What is disadvantage?
faq.shortAnswer: Disadvantage means you roll two d20s and use the lower result. It represents a difficult situation.

faq.question: What is a spell slot?
faq.shortAnswer: A spell slot is a limited resource many spellcasters spend to cast leveled spells. Your character sheet tracks how many you have.

faq.question: What is an NPC?
faq.shortAnswer: An NPC is a non-player character. The DM plays NPCs, such as villagers, villains, merchants, guards, and monsters that can talk.

faq.question: What is a house rule?
faq.shortAnswer: A house rule is a local table change or clarification. It applies to this group even if another table plays differently.

faq.question: Who decides the rules?
faq.shortAnswer: Official rules provide the base. The DM applies them during play, and this table's house rules explain local changes.

faq.question: What should I do if I am confused?
faq.shortAnswer: Ask the DM or another player. The best first step is to describe what your character wants to try.

glossary.plainLanguageDefinition: A roll to see whether a character succeeds at a task using one of the six abilities.
A number that shows how hard a character or creature is to hit. If an attack roll equals or beats the target's AC, the attack usually hits.
A roll to see whether an attack hits a target.
A smaller extra action some characters can take when a feature, spell, or rule says they can.
The ongoing story made from connected sessions, characters, places, and choices.
A contained story or mission. It may last one session or several sessions.
A top-down map used when movement, distance, cover, or positioning matters during an encounter.
The work the DM does before play, such as reading notes, choosing tools, preparing scenes, and deciding what needs a map.
A simple layout of a room, building, cave, ship, or other location. It is often useful for tactical scenes.
Custom material made by a DM, player, or community instead of official published rules.
A small figure used to represent a character, monster, or object on a physical or digital tabletop.
A prewritten adventure sold or shared for D&D. It usually provides story material, locations, monsters, maps, and guidance for the DM.
A short adventure intended to be played in one session, though some take longer.
A character already created for a player to use. Premade characters help a new group start faster when everyone is still learning character creation.
A setup meeting before Session 0 for technology, accounts, Discord, devices, and basic tool access.
Playing through the DM's description and the group's imagination instead of using a detailed map or miniatures.
A short first adventure used to teach the rules, table flow, tools, and character basics by playing.
A broad map that shows regions, travel routes, settlements, and where the party is in the larger world.
The fictional person a player controls in the game.
A character's main adventuring type, such as fighter, rogue, cleric, or wizard.
The record of a character's numbers, abilities, equipment, spells, and notes.
One pass through the turn order during combat, where each participant gets a turn.
Common dice named by how many sides they have. A d20 is the twenty-sided die used for many important rolls.
A loss of hit points caused by attacks, spells, hazards, or other dangers.
The target number for a check or saving throw. Meeting or beating it usually succeeds.
Rolling two d20s and using the lower result.
The person who runs the world, plays NPCs and monsters, applies rules, and keeps the game moving.
A focused scene or challenge, such as a fight, negotiation, trap, chase, or puzzle.
A number showing how much damage a character or creature can take before going down.
A local table rule that changes, clarifies, or adds to the official rules.
The order in which characters and enemies take turns during combat.
A major rest, usually overnight, that restores many resources.
A visual reference for locations, terrain, distances, tokens, and movement.
How far a character can usually move on their turn.
A non-player character controlled by the DM.
The group of player characters working together.
A real person who controls a character and makes choices for that character.
Training that lets a character add a bonus to certain rolls.
A special response that can happen outside your turn when a rule allows it.
Making choices, speaking, and acting as your character in the story.
A defensive roll to resist danger or reduce its effect.
One meeting of the group to play the campaign.
A setup meeting for characters, expectations, safety, schedule, and campaign tone.
Another name for Session Zero: the group setup meeting for characters, tone, rules, boundaries, and expectations.
A brief rest that lets some characters recover certain resources.
A trained area on the character sheet, such as Stealth, Perception, Arcana, or Persuasion.
A limited resource many spellcasters spend to cast leveled spells.
A marker on a virtual map showing a character, monster, object, or location.
A character's chance to act in initiative order.
A digital tool for maps, tokens, dice, and online tabletop play.

flow: The DM says combat has started.
Characters and enemies take turns from highest initiative to lowest.
On your turn, you usually can move and take one main action.
Some characters also have a bonus action.
Some situations allow a reaction outside your turn.
The DM tracks enemies and consequences.
Combat ends when the situation is resolved.

actions.name: Attack
actions.description: Use a weapon or attack ability against a target.

actions.name: Cast a Spell
actions.description: Use a spell your character knows or has prepared.

actions.name: Dash
actions.description: Move farther than usual on your turn.

actions.name: Disengage
actions.description: Move away carefully so nearby enemies usually cannot make opportunity attacks.

actions.name: Dodge
actions.description: Focus on defense and make yourself harder to hit until your next turn.

actions.name: Help
actions.description: Assist another character with a task or attack when the scene allows it.

actions.name: Hide
actions.description: Try to avoid being seen or noticed.

actions.name: Ready
actions.description: Prepare an action that will happen later if a specific trigger occurs.

actions.name: Search
actions.description: Look carefully for something hidden, unclear, or important.

actions.name: Use an Object
actions.description: Interact with an item, device, door, lever, potion, or other object.

characterSheetGuide.label: Class
characterSheetGuide.description: The character's main adventuring role, such as fighter, rogue, cleric, or wizard.

characterSheetGuide.label: Level
characterSheetGuide.description: A rough measure of character power and experience.

characterSheetGuide.label: Armor Class
characterSheetGuide.description: How hard you are to hit.

characterSheetGuide.label: Hit Points
characterSheetGuide.description: How much damage you can take before going down.

characterSheetGuide.label: Speed
characterSheetGuide.description: How far you can usually move on your turn.

characterSheetGuide.label: Abilities
characterSheetGuide.description: The six core traits: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma.

characterSheetGuide.label: Skills
characterSheetGuide.description: Things your character may be trained to do.

characterSheetGuide.label: Saving Throws
characterSheetGuide.description: Defensive rolls used to resist danger.

characterSheetGuide.label: Attacks
characterSheetGuide.description: Weapons or powers you can use to harm enemies.

characterSheetGuide.label: Spells
characterSheetGuide.description: Magical abilities, if your character has them.

characterSheetGuide.label: Features
characterSheetGuide.description: Special abilities from class, ancestry/species, background, or other choices.

whereToFindAnswers.destination: Use the character sheet tool chosen by the DM. It may be D&D Beyond, Roll20, a PDF, paper, or another tool.
Maps come later. Owlbear Rodeo or Roll20 will be linked when a campaign starts.

whereToFindAnswers.need: I need to know what happened last time
whereToFindAnswers.destination: Go to Sessions once the campaign has started.

whereToFindAnswers.need: I need the local table rules

whereToFindAnswers.destination: Use the official D&D free/basic rules source or whichever rules reference the DM approves.
Ask the DM. Describe what your character wants to try.

externalLearningResources.label: D&D Beyond Help
externalLearningResources.description: Character sheets, rules lookup, account help, and campaign tools.

externalLearningResources.label: Discord Support
externalLearningResources.description: Voice, text channels, account setup, and server basics.

externalLearningResources.label: Owlbear Rodeo Docs
externalLearningResources.description: Map rooms, tokens, scenes, and player basics.

externalLearningResources.label: Roll20 Help
externalLearningResources.description: Virtual tabletop, character sheets, maps, dice, and game setup.
